one_line_takeaway: A three-part empirical study showing that vision-language models aid autonomous-driving safety only when aligned to their strengths -- as semantic hazard screens and explicit human-authored behavioral constraints -- while naive injection of global VLM embeddings into a trajectory planner measurably degrades safety; it is a functional-safety, not a cybersecurity, paper.
key_finding: Representation-task alignment is decisive. (1) CLIP margin hazard screening works as a conservative screening layer but is brittle: it does well when the hazard causes a whole-scene change (low visibility Global tIoU 0.765) and worst for small/localized or temporal hazards (emergency scene 0.318, road debris 0.451); the Dual-Hazard fusion trades a little detection for far fewer false alarms (Video-TNR 0.70 vs 0.34). (2) Injecting global CLIP+DINOv2 embeddings into the planner made things WORSE on every metric -- ADE rose and RFS fell across all scenario categories -- a clean negative result showing semantic embeddings are not automatically useful control features. (3) Passenger-style language as an explicit behavioral CONSTRAINT delivered the clearest safety benefit: it suppressed rare catastrophic out-of-bounds/advance-when-should-stop failures (collapsing an absurd mean ADE of 6201 to single digits) and, after outlier filtering, the best-phrased instructions gave a modest 5.1% ADE improvement, mostly by making behavior more conservative in underspecified scenes. Prompt phrasing/grounding is itself a controllable safety variable (e.g. a construction prompt swap caused a 45.7% Global-tIoU drop; 9-12-word instructions worked best).
core_contribution: An empirical, system-level study (conference paper ESV26-224 by Greer et al., UC Merced Mi3 Lab / UC San Diego LISA) of WHERE and HOW vision-language (VLM) representations help autonomous-vehicle safety, conducted through three complementary case studies that the authors build themselves rather than a single new model. (1) A lightweight, category-agnostic open-vocabulary hazard-SCREENING signal built on CLIP image-text similarity margins (hazard prompt vs. negative prompt), evaluated on the COOOL benchmark. (2) An integration study injecting global scene-level CLIP/DINOv2 embeddings into a Motion-Transformer-style vision planner (MTR-VP) on the Waymo End-to-End Driving Dataset. (3) An instruction-conditioning study using passenger-style natural-language directives from doScenes injected into OpenEMMA's prompt. The unifying thesis (the paper's own argument, not cited work) is that VLM representations help most when used to EXPRESS semantic risk, intent, and behavioral constraints, and that naively injecting global VLM embeddings as generic planner features does not help and can hurt -- i.e., safe use of VLMs is an engineering/integration problem, not a modeling shortcut. NOTE: LingoQA, DriveLM, Talk2Car, DiLu, OpenEMMA, LMDrive, SimLingo, LaMPilot, RDA-Driver, AsyncDriver, COOOL, Lost-and-Found, SegmentMeIfYouCan are all CITED prior work (Related Research), not this paper's contributions; MTR, CLIP, DINOv2, ViT, and the doScenes/OpenEMMA datasets are external tools the authors reuse.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-security paper. 'Safety' here means functional/operational driving safety (avoiding collisions, staying in drivable regions, reasoning under ambiguity in open-world scenes), not cybersecurity, adversarial robustness, or attack defense. There is no threat model, adversary, or attack surface studied. The only security-adjacent remark is a single sentence in Limitations acknowledging the system is 'not tested under unsafe, contradictory, or malicious instructions' and that a real deployment would need a defined trust boundary and fallbacks for language-in-the-loop -- this is future work flagged, not an evaluated threat model. Cluster label 'Autonomous Vehicle Security' is a mismatch; no threat model is invented here.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: VLM-for-planning is studied only via direct conditioning, not object-level grounding, intermediate scene abstractions, or hierarchical planning that might exploit semantics better. doScenes instructions are retrospectively authored via the 'taxi test' heuristic, not real spoken passenger interactions, creating a distribution gap. All evaluation is OPEN-LOOP on logged nuScenes/Waymo clips where the logged ego behavior is already safe, so the planner never has to recover from compounding errors. All tested instructions are positive-goal-oriented; the system is untested under unsafe, contradictory, or malicious instructions, and no production trust boundary / arbitration policy is defined. Reviewer-noted additional gaps: hazard-screening thresholds are tuned on the same labeled subsets they are reported on (in-sample optimism), COOOL is only ~200 videos, prompt search was non-exhaustive, and CLIP lacks temporal reasoning (frames scored in isolation), which the authors flag as the cause of poor emergency-scene detection.

datasets_used: COOOL benchmark (~200 dashcam videos of unexpected road hazards; manually annotated hazard-visible vs. hazard-onset frames; grouped into 7 coarse hazard types) plus self-collected nominal suburban/urban/highway footage to balance to ~50/50 hazard/non-hazard (~34,000 frames each)
Waymo End-to-End Driving Dataset (WOD-E2E): multi-view 2D camera images, 5s ego kinematic history, high-level intent
doScenes (language-augmented extension of nuScenes, passenger directives via 'taxi test' heuristic) combined with the OpenEMMA planning framework (based on Waymo's EMMA)
quantitative_results: Hazard screening, CLIP ViT-L/14 Global tIoU (Table 1): low visibility 0.765 (positive tIoU 0.667), animal 0.657 (0.532), general 'hazard' 0.572 (negative tIoU 0.556), construction 0.563, airborne/falling 0.473, road debris 0.451, pedestrian 0.538, emergency scene worst 0.318 (positive tIoU only 0.037)
Fusion systems (Table 2): No-Hazard Global tIoU 0.596 / Video-TPR 0.96 / Video-TNR 0.34; With-Hazard 0.571 / 0.99 / 0.34; Dual-Hazard 0.589 / 0.92 / 0.70 (best false-alarm control)
Prompt sensitivity: construction 'road work ahead' vs 'a road with nothing unusual' = 0.564 Global tIoU, dropping to 0.305 with 'construction zone' vs 'a clear lane with no hazard' (45.7% decrease)
MTR-VP trajectory ADE top-1 (Table 3): base 1.4232 @3s / 3.3485 @5s; with CLIP+DINOv2 embeddings WORSE at 2.0142 @3s / 4.3379 @5s

MTR-VP Rater Feedback Score (Tables 4-5), base vs +CLIP/DINOv2: Overall 7.3433 vs 6.7862; Construction 7.7111 vs 6.9871; Intersection 7.6864 vs 6.7769; Pedestrian 7.5096 vs 7.1561; Cyclist 7.3138 vs 7.0224; Multi-Lane 7.2205 vs 6.3238; Debris 7.3794 vs 6.9512 (VLM-augmented lower on every category)

Instruction-conditioned OpenEMMA ADE (Table 6): visual-only baseline mean (All) 6201.443 vs with-instruction best 9.999 / avg 78.527 / worst 151.420; after Q97.5 outlier filter (~20 scenes removed) baseline 2.879 vs best 2.732 / avg 2.929 / worst 3.11; best-phrased instructions = 5.1% mean ADE reduction; 9-12-word instructions gave largest gains
baselines_compared: CLIP ViT-L/14 hazard-prompt-vs-negative-prompt margin with per-category tuned thresholds (three fusion variants: No-Hazard, With-Hazard, Dual-Hazard)
MTR-VP base vision planner vs MTR-VP augmented with global CLIP + DINOv2 scene embeddings
OpenEMMA vision-only (no-instruction) baseline vs doScenes instruction-conditioned OpenEMMA
